text: The surgeon uses a monopolar crown in coagulating mode to open the ovarian cortex of the normal left ovary.
The surgeon opens the ovarian cortex using a monopolar crown, initiating the cystectomy by applying traction and counter traction to the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon applies a small amount of monopolar energy to the ovary for a brief duration, visible as a controlled coagulation effect on the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the right ovarian dermoid cyst using two forceps, carefully manipulating the tissue to preserve fertility.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to increase the dissecting plane between the healthy ovary and the ovarian cyst, facilitating the separation of these structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the opposite side of the ovary, carefully maneuvering to avoid injury to the visible dermoid cyst.
The surgeon attempts to remove the entire dermoid cyst from the ovary without opening it, using careful traction and counter traction techniques.
The surgeon continues the dissection using traction and counter-traction, applying small bursts of energy to the ovary. The ovarian cyst is progressively being removed from its bed.

The surgeon continues the cystectomy, carefully dissecting along the correct plane to separate the ovarian cyst from the healthy ovary. Minimal energy is used to preserve ovarian follicles, and mechanical hemostasis is performed to control bleeding.
The surgeon uses operative forceps to detach the final attachment of the ovary from the cyst, completing the cystectomy. The cyst is removed without compromising the residual ovarian tissue.
The surgeon carefully avoids using bipolar coagulation on the residual ovary during the cystectomy, ensuring preservation of the ovarian tissue.
The surgeon performs hemostasis on a large six-centimeter dermoid cyst using a small stitch, ensuring the cyst is stabilized without opening it.
The surgeon attempts to restore the normal anatomy after completing the cystectomy, ensuring the residual ovary is preserved.
The surgeon places a stitch on the ovary without tying it, aiming to minimize the risk of ovarian ischemia.
The surgeon places a stitch to achieve an anatomically favorable result, ensuring proper alignment and positioning of the tissues.
The surgeon uses an intra-corporeal knotting technique to tie the knot, securing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon cuts the excess stitch and places a classical handbag suture, followed by inserting a twelve millimeter draw card.
The surgeon prepares for a protective ovarian cyst extraction, utilizing previously placed stitches to ensure anatomical integrity during the procedure.
The surgeon places the dermoid cyst inside a handbag-like device and prepares to extract it through the left iliac trochanter.
The surgeon transabdominally empties the cyst into a protective extraction bag. The emptied cyst is visible, showing the final aspect of the surgery.
